entries.summary: The 2026 GLP-1 patent cliff now spans India, China, Brazil, Canada, and Turkey — covering 40% of the world's population. China's semaglutide patent also expired in March 2026. Tirzepatide becomes eligible for US Paragraph IV challenges on May 13, 2026, though meaningful US generic competition remains unlikely before 2036 given Eli Lilly's 21-patent fortress.
entries.content: The 2026 GLP-1 patent cliff is more geographically concentrated than previously understood. India and China's semaglutide patents both expired in March 2026, while Brazil and Turkey expired earlier in 2026, and Canada in January 2026. Collectively, these markets cover approximately 40% of the world's population — creating a two-tier global GLP-1 market where innovator pricing applies in US/EU ($900-$1,350/month) but generic pricing dominates everywhere else ($15-$50/month). Dr. Reddy's plans to launch generic semaglutide in 87 countries in 2026, starting with Canada, India, Brazil, Turkey, and other emerging markets. A key milestone: tirzepatide (Mounjaro/Zepbound) becomes eligible for Paragraph IV patent challenges in the US on May 13, 2026 — exactly 4 years after Mounjaro's US approval date. However, Eli Lilly's tirzepatide patent fortress is formidable: 21 US patents cover the molecule, with the primary composition patent expiring January 2036 and formulation patents potentially extending protection to 2039-2041. No generic tirzepatide challenge has been filed as of March 2026, and meaningful US competition before 2036 is considered remote. The M&A angle: GeneOnline reports that the $100B+ GLP-1 patent cliff has triggered a global M&A race, with large pharma companies acquiring biosimilar capabilities and pipeline molecules to position for the post-exclusivity era. Russia's compulsory licensing approach, Brazil's rejection of Novo's patent extension request, and WHO's recommendations for tiered pricing and pooled procurement signal increasing government pressure on GLP-1 pricing globally.

entries.summary: The US District Court for the District of Delaware scheduled initial proceedings in Novo Nordisk's patent infringement suit against Teva over its biosimilar semaglutide filing.
entries.content: The US District Court for the District of Delaware scheduled initial proceedings in Novo Nordisk's patent infringement suit against Teva over its biosimilar semaglutide filing. Teva is challenging the core composition patent (US9,694,053) under Hatch-Waxman Paragraph IV, arguing obviousness over prior GLP-1 analogs. A decision is not expected before late 2027.

entries.summary: The Indian Patent Office heard arguments in the ongoing Section 3(d) challenge to Novo Nordisk's semaglutide patent. A ruling is expected by mid-2026.
entries.content: The Indian Patent Office heard arguments in the ongoing Section 3(d) challenge to Novo Nordisk's semaglutide patent. Generic manufacturers argued that semaglutide represents incremental modification of the known GLP-1 analog liraglutide without demonstrating significantly enhanced therapeutic efficacy as required under Indian patent law. A ruling is expected by mid-2026.

entries.summary: Semaglutide's patent landscape reveals a critical 6-year gap between India and US patent expiry: India's compound patent expired March 20, 2026 (strict 20-year term with no extensions), while US protection extends to December 2031 via Patent Term Adjustment and Extension.
entries.content: Semaglutide's patent landscape reveals a critical 6-year gap between India and US expiry. India follows a strict 20-year patent term with no extensions under TRIPS, leading to expiry on March 20, 2026. In the US, the main compound patent (US8,129,343) benefits from Patent Term Adjustment and Patent Term Extension, pushing expiry to December 2031. Novo Nordisk holds 154 granted US patents across its three semaglutide products (Ozempic, Wegovy, Rybelsus), creating a dense patent thicket. The company is projected to earn an estimated $166 billion from continued US exclusivity between 2026 and 2031, while markets covering 40% of the world's population open to generic competition in 2026.

entries.summary: India's Section 3(d) of the Patents Act prevented Novo Nordisk from evergreening its semaglutide patent, with the Delhi High Court allowing Dr. Reddy's to continue manufacturing and recognizing the challenge as prima facie valid.
entries.content: India's Section 3(d) of the Patents Act proved instrumental in enabling generic semaglutide competition. The Delhi High Court rejected Novo Nordisk's attempt to enforce a second patent, ruling that it represented potential double patenting and lacked true innovation under Section 3(d). Dr. Reddy's was allowed to continue manufacturing and exporting semaglutide. This decision builds on the precedent set by the landmark 2013 Novartis Glivec case and signals that India's patent framework strongly disfavors incremental modifications of known molecules. Over 40 Indian manufacturers capitalized on this ruling to prepare generic launches for the March 20, 2026 patent expiry.

entries.summary: Eli Lilly's tirzepatide (Mounjaro/Zepbound) is protected by 21 US patents with the primary compound patent expiring in January 2036, but formulation patents could extend protection as far as 2041.
entries.content: Eli Lilly's tirzepatide is shielded by a dense patent portfolio of 21 US patents and one FDA Regulatory Exclusivity. The key composition of matter patent (US9,474,780) expires in January 2036, while additional composition patents extend to September 2031 and January 2037. Formulation patents further restrict generic launch until potentially 2039-2041. Mounjaro becomes eligible for Paragraph IV patent challenges on May 13, 2026, though meaningful generic competition is not expected before 2036 at the earliest. This gives Eli Lilly a decade-long head start over generic competitors, contrasting sharply with semaglutide's more vulnerable patent position.

entries.summary: Teva and Galenicum Health successfully challenged Novo Nordisk's European semaglutide obesity patent at the EPO, which revoked the patent for lacking an inventive step, while additional challenges targeted oral semaglutide (Rybelsus) patents.
entries.content: Teva and Galenicum Health secured a major victory at the European Patent Office, which revoked Novo Nordisk's European patent on semaglutide use in a specific dosage for treating obesity, ruling it lacked an inventive step. Separately, the EPO's Boards of Appeal revoked two patents important for semaglutide tablet administration (Rybelsus) following challenges by Teva, Galenicum, Generics Ltd, and Hexal. In Canada, Sandoz, Apotex, and Teva filed generic applications immediately after patents expired on January 4, 2026. At least three US companies have filed Paragraph IV certifications, though settlements with Novo Nordisk suggest US generics remain unlikely before 2032.

entries.summary: Russia has already invoked compulsory licensing for semaglutide under Article 1360 of its Civil Code, while WHO's strategies for expanding GLP-1 access include both voluntary and compulsory licensing mechanisms for developing countries.
entries.content: Russia has taken the most aggressive approach to GLP-1 access, invoking compulsory licensing for semaglutide under Article 1360 of its Civil Code. Meanwhile, WHO's December 2025 obesity guideline explicitly recommends strategies including compulsory licensing, pooled procurement, and tiered pricing to expand access in developing countries. Brazil denied Novo Nordisk's request for patent extension, opening its market to generic competition from March 2026 with local manufacturer Biomm partnering with India's Biocon. In price-sensitive markets where patents expired in 2026 (India, China, Brazil, Turkey), monthly therapy costs are projected to fall to $40-50, compared to $300+ in the US. The patent cliff across markets covering 40% of world population marks the beginning of a structural price reset for GLP-1 therapies.
